items.title: I Built Something For You
items.body:
Most leaders don't see the drift until someone leaves.

They're in the room every day. They're running the meetings, making the calls, closing the deals. And the whole time, something is shifting underneath them that they can't feel because they're too close to it.

I've watched it happen to brilliant people. A founder who built a company from nothing starts losing his best leaders and genuinely doesn't understand why. A VP who used to be the one everyone trusted becomes the one everyone works around. A pastor who planted a church that changed lives wakes up and realizes his staff doesn't tell him the truth anymore.

It's never one big moment. It's a thousand small ones that nobody names.

I built something to name it.It's called The Validation Check™.

It's 9 questions. Takes about 2 minutes. It won't fix anything. But it will show you whether your culture is drifting, and it will put a number on what that drift is costing you.

I didn't build it to sell you something. I built it because the leaders I respect most are the ones willing to look at what's really happening, even when it's uncomfortable.

Two minutes. Be honest with yourself.

Take the Validation Check™
sightshift.com/validation-check?utm_source=river-email&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=validation-check-launch&utm_content=thu-apr9-announcement&utm_term=validation-check

Chris

items.title: I Built Something I Wish I'd Had 10 Years Ago
items.body:
I built something I wish I'd had 10 years ago.

After 15 years of working with leadership teams, I kept seeing the same thing. A CEO would call me and say, "Something's off. I can feel it. But I can't point to it."

By the time they could point to it, their best VP had already left. Or their culture had already calcified into something they didn't recognize. Or they'd spent six figures on a retreat that felt great for 48 hours and changed nothing.

The drift was never sudden. It was a thousand small moments nobody named.

So I built a way to name it.

It's called The Validation Check™ It's 9 questions. Takes about 2 minutes. It won't fix your culture. But it will tell you whether it's drifting, and it will put a dollar amount on what that drift is costing you.

I didn't build it to sell you something. I built it because the leaders I respect most are the ones who look at what's real before it becomes a crisis.

Two minutes. Be honest with yourself.

Link in comments.

batches.campaign: Dumb Theater Phase 2 — Michael the Motivational Speaker

items.title: The $40K Standing Ovation
items.body:
He flew in a speaker for $40,000. The guy was incredible. Standing ovation. People were hugging in the lobby afterward.

By Thursday, the VP of Sales was back to cc'ing the CEO on every email to cover himself. The ops director was back to running meetings like a courtroom. And the founder who booked the speaker? He was back to scanning the room for who agreed with him before he said what he actually thought.

The speaker didn't fail. The speaker was never going to reach what was actually driving those patterns.

You can pour the best fuel in the world into a car with a cracked engine block. It'll run hot for a minute. Then it stops.

That's what hype does. It floods the system with energy. But energy without a diagnosis just accelerates the same patterns. The VP doesn't need motivation to stop cc'ing the CEO. He needs to see why he does it in the first place. The ops director doesn't need a pep talk about collaboration. She needs to see what she's protecting.

The root is never skill. It's identity. And until something reaches the root, the standing ovation is just the opening act of a show that ends the same way every time.

This week, we're naming this pattern. It's one of the most expensive ones in leadership.

Chris
items.source: Composite coaching scenario + March 25 Live themes

items.title: She Felt It Slipping
items.subject_line: She felt it slipping by Wednesday
items.body:
She was the one crying at the leadership offsite. Not sad crying. Inspired crying. The kind where you scribble notes so fast you can barely read them later.

She told her team on the drive home, "This changes everything."

By Wednesday, she was yelling at her assistant for a scheduling mistake. By Friday, she couldn't figure out why her direct reports had stopped volunteering for projects again.

The event didn't break anything. But it didn't reach anything either.

Here's what most people don't see: motivation targets the 97% of your day when things are going fine. But your leadership is actually defined by the 3% when you're under pressure, when someone challenges your authority, when you're afraid you're about to be exposed.

That 3% runs on a different operating system. It doesn't run on personality. It doesn't run on intention. It doesn't run on hype. It runs on identity. And no keynote, no matter how good, rewires what happens when pressure hits.

I'm going live on YouTube tomorrow at noon ET to break this down. We're talking about why hype is not a plan, and what actually changes the patterns that show up when the energy fades. Join me for Lunch with Chris. Every Wednesday at noon ET on YouTube Live.Bring your lunch, whatever you're eating, and bring whatever's eating at you. We'll open up the chat for a live Q&A.

sightshift.com/lunchwithchris?utm_source=river-email&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=dumb-theater&utm_content=wk5-tue-story&utm_term=lunch-with-chris

Chris
items.cta: YouTube Live reminder — Lunch with Chris Wed noon ET
items.source: Composite coaching scenario + March 25 Live + Week 5 outline

items.title: The 3% That Runs Your Leadership
items.subject_line: It's the 3% that runs your leadership
items.body:
Most of your day, you're fine. Meetings go well. Decisions feel clear. You sound like the leader you want to be.

Then the 3% hits.

Someone pushes back on your idea in front of the team. A key hire tells you they're leaving. Your board asks a question you don't have the answer to.

In those moments, a different operating system takes over. The one running underneath everything else. The one shaped by fear, not skill.

That's why so much leadership development misses. It trains for the 97%. New communication frameworks. Better delegation models. Sharper strategic thinking. All useful. None of it touches what happens in the 3%.

The 3% is where you default. Where you prove your worth by overworking, or hide from conflict by going silent, or control the room because you're terrified of what happens if you don't.

The IFQ™ was built to measure what's driving that 3%. Not your personality. Not your strengths. The specific fears that take over when pressure hits, and the patterns they produce.

If you've ever walked out of a hard conversation and thought, "Why did I do that?" the answer lives in the 3%.

sightshift.com/ifq?utm_source=river-email&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=dumb-theater&utm_content=wk5-thu-framework&utm_term=ifq

Chris

items.title: 48 Hours After the Standing Ovation
items.body:
A CEO spent $40,000 on a speaker for his annual leadership summit.

The guy was electric. People cried. People hugged. Someone in accounting said it was the best day of their career.

48 hours later, the COO was back to running meetings like depositions. The sales VP was back to cc'ing the CEO on every email as a cover. And the CEO himself? He was back to making every decision alone because he was afraid his team would make the wrong one.

Nobody failed. The speaker was great. The team was engaged.

But you can't motivate your way past a fear you haven't named.

The COO runs meetings like depositions because she's terrified someone will challenge her competence. The VP cc's the CEO because he's afraid of being blamed. The CEO decides alone because letting go feels like losing control.

None of that changes in a keynote. It changes when you see the root.

Sound familiar?

items.title: Meet Michael the Motivational Speaker
items.body:
You've met this guy.

He walks into your company retreat with a wireless mic, a slide deck full of stock photos, and a story about climbing Kilimanjaro that somehow connects to quarterly revenue targets.

Your team leaves fired up. They use new words for a week. And then nothing changes. Because nothing was supposed to change. The goal was energy, not transformation.

Meet Michael. He's a character, but the pattern he represents is real. Companies spend billions on motivational speakers every year, not because they produce results, but because booking a speaker feels like doing something about your culture without actually doing anything about your culture.

It's the Taco Bell sauce of leadership development. It tastes familiar. It's easy to pour on. And it's not your product.

Tomorrow on Lunch with Chris, I'm breaking down why hype is not a plan, and what the gap between motivation and real change actually looks like. Noon ET on YouTube. Bring your lunch and bring your questions for the live Q&A.
items.source: Week 5 outline + March 25 Live (Taco Bell sauce metaphor)

items.title: Your Best People Aren't Leaving Because of Comp
items.body:
Your best people aren't leaving because of compensation.

They're leaving because of your environment.

Not the office layout. Not the perks. The invisible thing that happens in every meeting, every hallway conversation, every Slack thread. The thing no one names out loud but everyone adapts to.

Here's what I've seen after 15 years of working with leadership teams: the culture you think you have and the culture your people actually experience are almost never the same thing.

You think the culture is "open and honest." Your VP thinks the culture is "agree publicly, protect yourself privately." Your best engineer thinks the culture is "work hard, keep your head down, don't get noticed for the wrong reasons."

None of them are lying. They're all adapting to the same environment differently, based on what feels safe.

That's drift. It's happening right now, in your company, and you won't see it until someone you can't afford to lose walks out the door.

The question isn't whether your culture is drifting. It's whether you'll catch it before it costs you.

The Validation Check™ shows you where your culture actually is, not where you think it is. Takes 3 minutes.

www.thevalidationcheck.com?utm_source=linkedin&utm_medium=social&utm_campaign=dumb-theater&utm_content=wk5-wed-provocation&utm_term=validation-check
items.source: March 25 Live — environment shapes you, best people leaving

items.title: The 97/3 Principle
items.body:
There's a reason your team forgets everything from the leadership offsite within two weeks.

It's not because they don't care. It's because the offsite targeted the wrong layer.

Most leadership development addresses the 97% of your day when things are going fine. Better communication. Clearer delegation. Stronger strategic frameworks. All useful.

But your leadership is actually defined by the 3%.

The moment someone challenges your idea in front of the room. The meeting where your best performer tells you they're leaving. The call where you don't have the answer.

In those moments, a deeper operating system takes over. One built on fear, not training.

That operating system is why the VP agrees with everything in public and undermines it in private. It's why the ops director can't let go of control. It's why the founder scans the room for approval before saying what they actually think.

No keynote rewires that. No offsite reaches it.

It changes when you measure it. When you name the specific fears driving the patterns. When you stop treating the 97% and start diagnosing the 3%.

That's what the IFQ™ was built to do.

items.title: How Long Did the Feeling Last?
items.body:
What's the most fired-up you've ever been after a speaker or leadership event, and how long did it last?

I'll go first.

I sat in a conference in 2009 and took four pages of notes. I left ready to overhaul everything. I told my wife on the drive home I was going to change the way we ran our entire operation.

By the following Wednesday, I couldn't find the notebook.

The speaker wasn't the problem. The speaker was excellent. But what I needed wasn't energy. What I needed was someone to help me see the pattern I kept running back to every time pressure showed up.

Be honest in the comments. How long did the feeling last for you?
items.source: Chris personal storytelling + March 25 Live themes

items.title: Why does AI outperform your team?
items.subject_line: Why does AI outperform your team?
items.body:
I asked AI to build a pitch deck for a mid-size company last month.

It came back in four minutes. Detailed. Specific. Tailored to the company's size, industry, and stated goals. The kind of deliverable that would take a consulting team two weeks and a five-figure retainer.

It was better than 95% of what I've seen come out of actual leadership teams.

That stopped me. Not because AI is impressive. Because it raised a question most leaders aren't asking.

If a machine with no soul, no relationships, no skin in the game can outperform the people you've invested years developing, what does that tell you about your people?

Here's what it tells me: it's not a skill gap. It's an identity gap.

AI doesn't get insecure. It doesn't prove its worth by overworking. It doesn't hide from hard conversations. It doesn't default to controlling the room because it's afraid of what happens if it doesn't.

Your people do. And that's not a training problem. It's a leadership culture problem. The kind no tool or motivational speaker will fix.

I'm teaching a workshop on this. One afternoon. Thirty leaders. Columbus, Ohio.

Why AI Outperforms Your Team, and What That Reveals About Your Leadership
Wednesday, May 6 | 2:00–4:00 PM + Networking
Summit | Columbus, OH
$135 per seat | 30 seats only

This isn't a tech talk. I won't teach you how to use AI. I'll teach you why it exposes the identity crisis hiding inside your leadership culture, and what to do about it.

And then you will engage in small-group table conversations with other leaders about how it's showing up in your team. You'll leave with a concrete takeaway you can apply within a week.

My friend Chris Orecchio from C12 Columbus is co-hosting. If you lead a team and you've felt that gap between what your people are capable of and what they actually produce under pressure, this is the room to be in.

Thirty seats. That's it.

sightshift.com/columbus?utm_source=campaign-email&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=columbus-ai-event&utm_content=wk1-announcement&utm_term=columbus-event
Chris
P.S. Not in Columbus? Reply to this email and let us know if you want to bring this event to your city. We're making plans to take this conversation to more places later this year.
items.source: Columbus AI Event planning doc + event page
